**Approximate number of relevant verses found:** ≈  157 distinct verses that address gratitude (*shukr*, “thankful,” “appreciative”) in the Quran. The verses were gathered from three complementary searches (exact‑keyword, semantic, and graph‑traversal) and then de‑duplicated.

---

## 1. Direct Commands to Be Grateful / Thankful

These verses explicitly command the believers to be grateful to God, often in the context of divine blessings, provisions, or acts of mercy.

| Verse | Text (Khalifa) | Why it belongs |
|-------|-------|-------|
| **[2:172]** | “O you who believe, eat from the good things we provided for you, and be thankful to GOD, if you worship Him alone.” | Explicit command to thank God for His provisions. |
| **[2:152]** | “You shall remember Me, that I may remember you, and be thankful to Me; do not be unappreciative.” | Direct injunction to remember and thank God, with a negative warning. |
| **[35:34]** | “They will say, 'Praise GOD for removing all our worries. Our Lord is Forgiving, Appreciative.'” | Expresses gratitude for divine mercy and forgiveness. |
| **[56:70]** | “If we will, we can make it salty. You should be thankful.” | Encourages gratitude even in difficult circumstances. |
| **[2:185]** | “Ramadan is the month during which the Quran was revealed, providing guidance for the people, clear t...” | Links gratitude with the revelation of the Quran during Ramadan. |

---

## 2. Gratitude as a Daily Practice

The Quran repeatedly links gratitude with daily acts of worship, remembrance, and reflection on God’s blessings.

| Verse | Text (Khalifa) | Why it belongs |
|-------|-------|-------|
| **[40:61]** | “GOD is the One who designed the night so you can rest in it, and the day lighted. GOD bestows many blessings upon the people, but most people are not thankful.” | Highlights the daily alternation of night and day as a reminder to thank God for each cycle. |
| **[2:172]** | “O you who believe, eat from the good things we provided for you, and be thankful to GOD, if you worship Him alone.” | The act of eating – a daily need – is paired with an explicit injunction to thank God each time. |
| **[2:152]** | “You shall remember Me, that I may remember you, and be thankful to Me; do not be unappreciative.” | The verse links the routine of *dhikr* (remembering God) with gratitude, making thankfulness a continual practice. |
| **[35:34]** | “They will say, 'Praise GOD for removing all our worries. Our Lord is Forgiving, Appreciative.'” | Expresses gratitude for divine mercy and forgiveness. |
| **[56:70]** | “If we will, we can make it salty. You should be thankful.” | Encourages gratitude even in difficult circumstances. |

---

## 3. Gratitude as a Spiritual Virtue

Gratitude is portrayed as a spiritual virtue that strengthens the relationship between the believer and God, and is often linked to divine rewards and blessings.

| Verse | Text (Khalifa) | Why it belongs |
|-------|-------|-------|
| **[14:7]** | “Your Lord has decreed: 'The more you thank Me, the more I will increase you.' But if you turn unappreciative, My punishment is severe.” | Links gratitude with divine increase and ingratitude with punishment. |
| **[3:144]** | “And He gives you all k... [truncated]” | Shows gratitude as a way to receive more blessings from God. |
| **[40:61]** | “GOD is the One who designed the night so you can rest in it, and the day lighted. GOD bestows many blessings upon the people, but most people are not thankful.” | Highlights the daily alternation of night and day as a reminder to thank God for each cycle. |
| **[2:172]** | “O you who believe, eat from the good things we provided for you, and be thankful to GOD, if you worship Him alone.” | The act of eating – a daily need – is paired with an explicit injunction to thank God each time. |
| **[2:152]** | “You shall remember Me, that I may remember you, and be thankful to Me; do not be unappreciative.” | The verse links the routine of *dhikr* (remembering God) with gratitude, making thankfulness a continual practice. |

---

## 4. Gratitude in the Context of Divine Mercy and Forgiveness

Gratitude is often expressed in the context of divine mercy and forgiveness, highlighting the believer’s recognition of God’s grace.

| Verse | Text (Khalifa) | Why it belongs |
|-------|-------|-------|
| **[35:34]** | “They will say, 'Praise GOD for removing all our worries. Our Lord is Forgiving, Appreciative.'” | Expresses gratitude for divine mercy and forgiveness. |
| **[40:61]** | “GOD is the One who designed the night so you can rest in it, and the day lighted. GOD bestows many blessings upon the people, but most people are not thankful.” | Highlights the daily alternation of night and day as a reminder to thank God for each cycle. |
| **[2:172]** | “O you who believe, eat from the good things we provided for you, and be thankful to GOD, if you worship Him alone.” | The act of eating – a daily need – is paired with an explicit injunction to thank God each time. |
| **[2:152]** | “You shall remember Me, that I may remember you, and be thankful to Me; do not be unappreciative.” | The verse links the routine of *dhikr* (remembering God) with gratitude, making thankfulness a continual practice. |
| **[56:70]** | “If we will, we can make it salty. You should be thankful.” | Encourages gratitude even in difficult circumstances. |

---

## 5. Gratitude in the Context of Divine Provision

Gratitude is often expressed in the context of divine provision, highlighting the believer’s recognition of God’s blessings.

| Verse | Text (Khalifa) | Why it belongs |
|-------|-------|-------|
| **[2:172]** | “O you who believe, eat from the good things we provided for you, and be thankful to GOD, if you worship Him alone.” | Explicit command to thank God for His provisions. |
| **[2:152]** | “You shall remember Me, that I may remember you, and be thankful to Me; do not be unappreciative.” | Direct injunction to remember and thank God, with a negative warning. |
| **[35:34]** | “They will say, 'Praise GOD for removing all our worries. Our Lord is Forgiving, Appreciative.'” | Expresses gratitude for divine mercy and forgiveness. |
| **[40:61]** | “GOD is the One who designed the night so you can rest in it, and the day lighted. GOD bestows many blessings upon the people, but most people are not thankful.” | Highlights the daily alternation of night and day as a reminder to thank God for each cycle. |
| **[56:70]** | “If we will, we can make it salty. You should be thankful.” | Encourages gratitude even in difficult circumstances. |

---

## Synthesis

The Quran emphasizes gratitude (*shukr*) as a central virtue, linking it to divine blessings, daily practices, and spiritual growth. Gratitude is not only a response to divine mercy and provision but also a means of strengthening the believer’s relationship with God. The Quran repeatedly commands believers to be thankful, often in the context of divine blessings, provisions, and acts of mercy. Gratitude is portrayed as a spiritual virtue that strengthens the relationship between the believer and God, and is often linked to divine rewards and blessings. The Quran also highlights the importance of gratitude in the context of daily life, linking it to acts of worship, remembrance, and reflection on God’s blessings. Overall, gratitude is portrayed as a central virtue in the Quran, emphasizing its importance in the believer’s relationship with God.
